original_abstract: All plants inevitably absorb Na⁺ when exposed to saline environments. Once it enters the symplast, Na⁺ is partitioned both within individual cells and across various tissues and organs. While this accumulation can assist in lowering cellular water potential, it also carries the risk of severe toxicity. Consequently, the precise control of Na⁺ fluxes is essential for survival. Increasing evidence suggests that the distinction between salt-tolerant and salt-sensitive species does not stem from different types of transporters, but rather from how effectively they regulate uptake and internal distribution. Recent research has identified a diverse array of regulatory mechanisms involving calcium signaling, cyclic nucleotides, reactive oxygen species, and hormonal pathways, as well as complex transcriptional and post-translational modifications. This review provides a comprehensive overview of intra- and intercellular Na⁺ movement and summarizes our current understanding of how these fluxes are orchestrated during the initial stages of salt stress.
humanized_abstract: All plants are inevitably exposed to sodium ions (Na⁺) when facing salinity stress. Sodium ions are then distributed within the symplast at the cell level and at the organ level. Even though sodium ions contribute to decrease the cellular water potential, their accumulation is still dangerous and must be tightly regulated. While the differences between salt tolerant and salt sensitive species remain poorly explained and are not necessarily linked to the type of transporters used, the regulatory capacity on uptake and internal deposition has been shown to differ between species. Recent research suggests that salt tolerance can be regulated at a huge number of levels, ranging from calcium signaling and cyclic nucleotides and reactive oxygen species signaling, as well as hormone regulation at the transcriptional and post-transcriptional levels. The aim of this review is to give an exhaustive view of the intra and intercellular Na⁺ dynamics, especially focusing on the regulatory aspects governing salt tolerance in the early phases of salt shock.
